title: Air-fryer beef burgers
subtitle: Juicy patties cooked through in 12 minutes
excerpt: Beef mince shaped into patties, seasoned, and cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for 12 minutes. The air fryer is a better method than a pan for burgers cooked from fresh mince because the fat drips away from the patty rather than pooling around it, producing a less greasy result.
sourceNotes: The beef burger is an American invention that became part of everyday British cooking from the 1970s through fast-food restaurants. Home-cooked versions are almost always better than fast-food equivalents because the mince can be 20% fat, which is the minimum needed for a juicy result. The air fryer cooks burgers more evenly than a dry pan and without the smoke of a very hot grill.

content.text: Do not press the patties during cooking. Pressing a beef burger while it cooks squeezes out the fat and juices that keep it moist. The air fryer does not need any pressing to cook the patty through: the circulating heat reaches all sides equally.

Mix the {{beef-mince}} with {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{worcestershire-sauce}} if using. Handle as little as possible: overworking the mince makes the patty dense and tough. Divide into four equal portions and shape into patties about 2 cm thick. Make a small indent in the centre of each with your thumb: this prevents the burger from puffing up during cooking. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C for 3 minutes. Place the patties in the basket, not touching. Cook at 200°C for 6 minutes on the first side, flip carefully, and cook for a further 5 to 6 minutes. Rest for 2 minutes. The internal temperature should be 71°C for fully cooked mince.

items.fix: Refrigerating for 30 minutes sets the fat and helps the patty hold its shape. Use mince with at least 15 to 20% fat: very lean mince produces crumbly, dry burgers
items.cause: Patties not chilled before cooking, or mince with insufficient fat

items.fix: The thumb indent compensates for the puffing that happens as the proteins contract. Press firmly into the centre of each patty before chilling
items.cause: No thumb indent in the centre before cooking
items.symptom: Burger puffed up like a meatball, not flat

items.fix: Keep patties to 2 cm thick. Thicker patties need a lower temperature: reduce to 180°C and cook for 16 minutes total
items.cause: Patty too thick for the 200°C air fryer heat
items.symptom: Burger exterior dry before centre is cooked through

content.text: Air-fryer burgers are a Friday-night staple for families. Serve in toasted buns with sliced cheese melted on in the last minute of cooking, lettuce, tomato, and your choice of sauce. The air fryer produces less mess than a griddle pan and no smoke.
